name: Slovakia
tagline: From Great Moravia to the heart of Europe, a nation that waited a thousand years for its own state.
summary: Slovakia's history is the story of a Slavic people who held the same Carpathian uplands for more than a millennium while almost never ruling themselves. Their lands lay at the core of ninth-century Great Moravia, centred partly on Nitra, where the Byzantine missionaries Cyril and Methodius created a Slavonic liturgy and the first Slavic script. After Great Moravia fell to the Magyars, the Slovak lands were absorbed around the year 1000 into the Kingdom of Hungary, where they remained for roughly a thousand years as 'Upper Hungary', and during the Ottoman wars Bratislava (Pressburg) served as the kingdom's capital and coronation city. A national awakening in the nineteenth century, crowned by Ľudovít Štúr's codification of a Slovak literary language in the 1840s, gave the people a modern identity in the face of Magyarization. Statehood came only in 1918, when Slovaks joined the Czechs in Czechoslovakia; a clerical-authoritarian Slovak State allied to Nazi Germany followed in 1939, the deportation of Slovak Jews and the Slovak National Uprising of 1944, then restoration, communism and federalization. The Velvet Revolution of 1989 ended communist rule, and the peaceful 'Velvet Divorce' created an independent Slovakia on 1 January 1993. It joined NATO and the European Union in 2004 and adopted the euro in 2009.

founding.label: The Velvet Divorce and an independent Slovakia
founding.detail: Independent Slovakia was born on 1 January 1993, when Czechoslovakia separated peacefully into the Czech Republic and Slovakia in the so-called Velvet Divorce, the only nonviolent dissolution of a state in post-war Europe. After the June 1992 elections exposed deep differences between Czech and Slovak leaders over the pace of market reform and the shape of the federation, the two governments agreed to part rather than hold a referendum. The new Slovak Republic, with its capital at Bratislava, was the first fully sovereign Slovak state in history, even though the Slovak lands had been a distinct community for more than a thousand years, at the core of Great Moravia in the ninth century and then part of the Kingdom of Hungary from around 1000. Within little more than a decade the young republic joined NATO and the European Union in 2004 and adopted the euro in 2009.

quickFacts.label: Early heartland
quickFacts.value: Great Moravia, 9th century, centred partly on Nitra

quickFacts.label: Within Hungary
quickFacts.value: Absorbed into the Kingdom of Hungary c. 1000, for roughly a thousand years as 'Upper Hungary'

quickFacts.label: Literary language
quickFacts.value: Codified by Ľudovít Štúr from central Slovak dialects in the 1840s

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Velvet Divorce; Slovak Republic born 1 January 1993

quickFacts.label: European integration
quickFacts.value: Joined NATO and the EU in 2004; adopted the euro on 1 January 2009

eras.title: Great Moravia and the Mission of Cyril and Methodius
eras.standfirst: The Slovak lands lay at the heart of the first great Slavic state, where two Byzantine brothers created a written language for the Slavs before the Magyars swept it away.
body: In the early ninth century the Slavs of the middle Danube formed two neighbouring polities: the Principality of Nitra, in what is now western Slovakia, and the Principality of Moravia to the west. Around 833 the Moravian prince Mojmír I drove out Pribina, the ruler of Nitra, and joined the two into a single realm that historians call Great Moravia, the first powerful Slavic state of central Europe. Under Mojmír and his successors the new principality grew strong enough to resist the pressure of the East Frankish empire on its western frontier.

Seeking to free his church from Frankish and Latin tutelage, Prince Rostislav appealed to Constantinople, and in 863 the Byzantine emperor sent the brothers Constantine, later known as Cyril, and Methodius. The two missionaries devised the Glagolitic alphabet, the first script tailored to a Slavic tongue, and translated the scriptures and liturgy into what became Old Church Slavonic, the language of a Slavic Christianity. Their work, conducted partly from Nitra and the Moravian heartland, gave the Slavs a written culture of their own and is celebrated as a founding act of Slovak and wider Slavic civilisation.

Great Moravia reached its height under Svatopluk I, who reigned from about 870 to 894 and won recognition of his standing from the papacy, extending his power across much of central Europe. After his death the realm fractured among his sons and was worn down by warfare with the Franks. As the semi-nomadic Magyars pushed into the Carpathian Basin, Great Moravia disintegrated, and a Magyar victory in 906–907 is conventionally taken as the end of the state, opening the way for the Slovak lands to be drawn into a new Hungarian kingdom.

events.title: Founding of Great Moravia
events.summary: Prince Mojmír I expelled Pribina of Nitra and united the principalities of Moravia and Nitra into Great Moravia, the first powerful Slavic state of central Europe, with the Slovak lands at its core.

events.title: Mission of Cyril and Methodius
events.summary: At Prince Rostislav's request the Byzantine brothers Cyril and Methodius arrived in Great Moravia, devised the Glagolitic script and translated the liturgy into Old Church Slavonic, founding a written Slavic culture.

events.title: Reign of Svatopluk I
events.summary: Under Svatopluk I Great Moravia reached its greatest extent and won recognition from the papacy, becoming the dominant power of the middle Danube before the state fragmented after his death.

events.title: Fall of Great Moravia to the Magyars
events.summary: Weakened by internal strife and Frankish wars, Great Moravia collapsed as the Magyars occupied the Carpathian Basin; a Magyar victory in 906–907 is taken to mark the end of the state.

figures.name: Saints Cyril and Methodius
figures.blurb: Sent from Constantinople in 863, the brothers Cyril and Methodius created the Glagolitic alphabet and translated the scriptures and liturgy into Old Church Slavonic, giving the Slavs their first written language and church.

figures.name: Svatopluk I
figures.blurb: The greatest ruler of Great Moravia, Svatopluk expanded the realm to its widest extent and secured recognition of his independent standing from Pope John VIII, but his state did not long survive his death.

pullquote.text: The mission of Cyril and Methodius in 863 gave the Slavs the Glagolitic script and a liturgy in their own tongue, a founding moment of Slavic literate culture.
pullquote.attribution: On the Slavonic mission to Great Moravia

eras.title: A Thousand Years in the Kingdom of Hungary
eras.standfirst: Absorbed into the Hungarian kingdom around the year 1000, the Slovak lands became 'Upper Hungary', and in the Ottoman age Bratislava rose to be the kingdom's capital and coronation city.
body: After the fall of Great Moravia the Slovak lands were drawn around the year 1000 into the new Kingdom of Hungary, founded by King Stephen I. For roughly the next thousand years the Slovaks lived under the Hungarian crown, their territory known as 'Upper Hungary' or simply 'the Highlands'. The Hungarian state ruled the multi-ethnic region without recognising the Slovaks as a separate nation, identifying them less by their Slavic speech than by the lands they inhabited.

The lands of Upper Hungary were not a backwater. German settlers and royal charters fostered prosperous mining and trading towns, and in 1467 the country's first university, the Universitas Istropolitana, was founded at Pressburg. The Ottoman advance transformed the region's standing: after the Turkish conquest of central Hungary the parliament in 1536 raised Pressburg, today's Bratislava, to capital of the truncated kingdom, near the imperial residence of Vienna and protected by its fortifications.

For nearly three centuries Pressburg was the political and ceremonial heart of Habsburg Hungary. Its St Martin's Cathedral became the coronation church of the kingdom: between 1563 and 1830 a succession of Hungarian kings and royal consorts were crowned there, among them Maria Theresa in 1741, who made the city a centre of her eastern realm. Only after the reconquest of Buda and the eighteenth-century recovery did the capital's functions begin to drift back toward the Hungarian heartland.

events.title: Incorporation into the Kingdom of Hungary
events.summary: The Slovak lands were absorbed into the Kingdom of Hungary founded by Stephen I, beginning roughly a thousand years under the Hungarian crown as the region known as 'Upper Hungary'.

events.title: Universitas Istropolitana at Pressburg
events.summary: Slovakia's first university, the Universitas Istropolitana, was founded at Pressburg, an early sign of the towns' wealth and learning within the Hungarian kingdom.

events.title: Pressburg made capital of Hungary
events.summary: As the Ottomans overran central Hungary, the parliament promoted Pressburg (now Bratislava) to capital of the Kingdom of Hungary, a secure stronghold close to Habsburg Vienna.

events.title: Pressburg the coronation city
events.summary: Between 1563 and 1830, eleven Hungarian kings and eight royal consorts were crowned at St Martin's Cathedral in Pressburg, including Maria Theresa in 1741, making the city the ceremonial heart of the kingdom.

figures.name: Maria Theresa
figures.role: Archduchess of Austria and Queen of Hungary
figures.blurb: Crowned Queen of Hungary at St Martin's Cathedral in Pressburg in 1741, Maria Theresa made the city a centre of her eastern kingdom during a long reign that reshaped the Habsburg lands the Slovaks inhabited.

figures.name: Stephen I of Hungary
figures.blurb: The founder and first crowned king of the Christian Kingdom of Hungary around the year 1000, whose new state absorbed the Slovak lands of former Great Moravia into a realm that would hold them for a millennium.

pullquote.text: For roughly a thousand years the Slovak lands were 'Upper Hungary', and in the Ottoman age Pressburg was the kingdom's capital and coronation city.

eras.title: The Slovak National Awakening
eras.standfirst: Against the pressure of Magyarization, scholars and patriots forged a modern Slovak nation around a literary language codified by Ľudovít Štúr.
body: By the late eighteenth century the Slovaks, long defined only as inhabitants of Upper Hungary, began to reach for a distinct identity. The first stirrings were religious and linguistic: the Catholic priest Anton Bernolák attempted in the 1780s to fashion a written Slovak from western dialects, while Protestant patriots looked to Czech and to the memory of Great Moravia, around which a heavily mythologised national story would later be built.

The decisive step came in the 1840s. A Lutheran scholar and poet, Ľudovít Štúr, and his circle resolved in 1843 to base a single Slovak literary standard on the central Slovak dialects, a language that could unite speakers of many regional tongues. Štúr set out its grammar and argued its case in print, and his standard, refined by later grammarians, was accepted as the modern Slovak literary language. The codification gave the awakening a tool that no government could easily suppress, anchoring a sense of nationhood in a shared tongue.

Yet political room was narrowing. After the Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867 the Hungarian half of the monarchy pursued increasingly aggressive policies of Magyarization, pressing the Magyar language on schools and public life and refusing the Slovaks recognition as a nation. Slovak cultural institutions were curtailed, and many emigrated, especially to the United States, where expatriate communities would later play a part in the creation of a Czechoslovak state.

events.title: Bernolák's first written Slovak
events.summary: The Catholic priest Anton Bernolák codified an early written Slovak based on western dialects, an opening move in the national awakening that the next generation would supersede.

events.title: Štúr codifies literary Slovak
events.summary: Ľudovít Štúr and his associates resolved to base a unified Slovak literary language on the central Slovak dialects; his standard, refined by later grammarians, became modern literary Slovak.

events.title: Magyarization under the Compromise
events.summary: After the Austro-Hungarian Compromise the Hungarian government intensified Magyarization, pressing the Magyar language on Slovak schools and public life and denying the Slovaks recognition as a nation.

events.title: Slovaks in the revolutions of 1848
events.summary: During the revolutions of 1848 Slovak leaders, Štúr among them, set out national demands and raised volunteers, asserting the Slovaks as a distinct people within the Hungarian kingdom.

figures.name: Ľudovít Štúr
figures.role: Codifier of the Slovak literary language
figures.blurb: Scholar, poet and politician, Štúr led the Slovak national revival and in the 1840s codified a literary language from the central Slovak dialects, the basis of modern standard Slovak and a foundation of national identity.

figures.name: Anton Bernolák
figures.blurb: A Catholic priest who in the 1780s produced the first systematic attempt at a written Slovak, drawn from western dialects; though later superseded by Štúr's standard, his work began the modern history of the Slovak language.

pullquote.text: In the 1840s Ľudovít Štúr and his circle built a literary Slovak from the central dialects, giving the awakening a language that government could not easily suppress.

eras.title: Czechoslovakia, the Slovak State and the Uprising
eras.standfirst: Statehood arrived inside Czechoslovakia, was perverted into a wartime clerical regime allied to Hitler, and was reclaimed in the Slovak National Uprising of 1944.
body: When Austria-Hungary collapsed at the end of the First World War, the Slovaks found their first state by joining the Czechs. The Pittsburgh Agreement of 1918, signed by Czech and Slovak émigrés in the United States, envisaged a common republic with Slovak autonomy; Czechoslovakia was declared in Prague on 28 October 1918, and two days later the Slovak National Council endorsed the union in the Martin Declaration of 30 October 1918. The interwar republic was the most democratic and prosperous state in the region, yet many Slovaks chafed at centralisation from Prague and the promised autonomy never fully materialised.

The dismemberment of Czechoslovakia by Nazi Germany opened a darker chapter. On 14 March 1939 a separate Slovak State was proclaimed under the Catholic priest Jozef Tiso, a clerical-authoritarian regime that became a client and ally of Hitler's Germany. Its government enacted harsh anti-Jewish laws and, beginning in March 1942, made Slovakia the first of Germany's allies to consent to the deportation of its Jews: between March and October 1942 some 57,000 Slovak Jews were deported to German-occupied Poland, the great majority of whom were murdered, and further deportations followed in 1944–45. In all more than 70,000 Jews from Slovakia were deported and more than 60,000 killed.

Resistance gathered against the regime and its German patrons. On 29 August 1944 the Slovak National Uprising broke out, centred on Banská Bystrica, where the Slovak National Council and a Free Slovak Radio took control of much of the country and tens of thousands of soldiers and partisans took up arms. German forces poured in to crush the rising; Banská Bystrica fell on 27 October 1944, and savage reprisals followed, with thousands killed and villages destroyed. Though defeated militarily, the uprising became a defining act of Slovak resistance and a foundation of the post-war state's legitimacy.

events.title: Czechoslovakia and the Martin Declaration
events.summary: Czechoslovakia was declared in Prague on 28 October 1918; on 30 October the Slovak National Council endorsed the union in the Martin Declaration, giving the Slovaks their first modern statehood alongside the Czechs.

events.title: Proclamation of the Slovak State
events.summary: As Germany dismembered Czechoslovakia, a separate Slovak State was proclaimed under the priest Jozef Tiso, a clerical-authoritarian regime that became a client and ally of Nazi Germany.

events.title: Deportation of Slovak Jews
events.summary: Slovakia became the first Axis partner to consent to the deportation of its Jews; between March and October 1942 some 57,000 Slovak Jews were deported to German-occupied Poland, with more than 60,000 of Slovakia's Jews murdered overall.

events.title: Slovak National Uprising
events.summary: The Slovak National Uprising broke out at Banská Bystrica against the Tiso regime and German occupation; after two months of fighting the rising was crushed on 27 October 1944 amid brutal reprisals.

figures.name: Milan Rastislav Štefánik
figures.blurb: A Slovak astronomer, general and diplomat, Štefánik worked with Masaryk and Beneš to win Allied support for a Czechoslovak state and to raise its legions abroad, becoming a national hero before his death in an air crash in 1919.

figures.name: Jozef Tiso
figures.role: President of the wartime Slovak State
figures.blurb: A Catholic priest and leader of the Slovak People's Party, Tiso headed the clerical-authoritarian Slovak State allied to Nazi Germany, under which Slovakia deported tens of thousands of Jews. He was tried and executed for treason and collaboration in 1947.

pullquote.text: Slovakia became the first of Germany's allies to consent to the deportation of its Jews; more than 70,000 were deported and more than 60,000 murdered.

eras.title: Communism, the Velvet Revolution and the Velvet Divorce
eras.standfirst: Restored Czechoslovakia fell under Soviet-bloc communism, gained a federal Slovak republic in 1968, threw off one-party rule in 1989 and then parted peacefully into two states.
body: Liberated from German occupation, Czechoslovakia was restored but soon drawn into the Soviet sphere; from 1948 the Communist Party ruled a one-party state under Moscow's domination. For Slovaks the most lasting structural change came with the reforms of 1968. The Prague Spring's liberalisation was crushed by the Warsaw Pact invasion of August 1968, but its one surviving measure was the Constitutional Law of Federation of 28 October 1968, which from 1 January 1969 turned the centralised state into a federation of a Czech and a Slovak Socialist Republic, finally giving the Slovaks a republic of their own name, even if the subsequent 'normalisation' under Gustáv Husák kept real power centralised in the party.

Communist rule ended with the Velvet Revolution. It began with a student demonstration in Bratislava on 16 November 1989 and the larger march in Prague the following day, and over six weeks of mass protests, with Public Against Violence mobilising Slovaks and Civic Forum the Czechs, the Communist Party surrendered its monopoly. The transition was almost entirely peaceful, and by the end of 1989 a democratic Czechoslovakia was taking shape.

Democracy quickly exposed the strains between the two nations. The June 1992 elections returned leaders with sharply different visions, the Czech side favouring rapid market reform and a strong central state, the Slovak side, under Vladimír Mečiar, seeking a slower transition and greater sovereignty. Rather than fight, the two governments negotiated a separation. On 1 January 1993 Czechoslovakia dissolved into the Czech Republic and Slovakia in the 'Velvet Divorce', a peaceful split that gave the Slovaks, after more than a thousand years, a fully independent state of their own.

events.title: Communist takeover
events.summary: The Communist Party seized full power in Czechoslovakia, establishing a one-party state under Soviet domination that would last for four decades.

events.title: Federalization of Czechoslovakia
events.summary: After the Prague Spring was crushed, the Constitutional Law of Federation of 28 October 1968 created, from 1 January 1969, a Slovak Socialist Republic within a federal Czechoslovakia, the Slovaks' first republic in name.

events.title: The Velvet Revolution
events.summary: Beginning with a student march in Bratislava on 16 November and protests in Prague the next day, six weeks of demonstrations led the Communist Party to give up its monopoly, ending one-party rule peacefully.

events.title: The Velvet Divorce
events.summary: Czechoslovakia split peacefully into the Czech Republic and Slovakia on 1 January 1993, creating, after more than a thousand years, the first fully independent Slovak state.

figures.name: Alexander Dubček
figures.blurb: A Slovak who as Communist Party leader launched the 1968 Prague Spring's 'socialism with a human face'; ousted after the Soviet invasion, he re-emerged in 1989 as a symbol of the Velvet Revolution and chaired the federal parliament.

figures.name: Vladimír Mečiar
figures.role: First Prime Minister of independent Slovakia
figures.blurb: The dominant Slovak politician of the early 1990s, Mečiar led the Slovak negotiations that produced the Velvet Divorce and became prime minister of the new republic, though his later authoritarian style delayed Slovakia's entry into NATO.

pullquote.text: The Velvet Divorce of 1 January 1993 was the only nonviolent dissolution of a state in post-war Europe, and it gave the Slovaks their first sovereign state.
pullquote.attribution: On the peaceful split of Czechoslovakia

eras.title: Independent Slovakia in Europe
eras.standfirst: The young republic anchored itself in the West, settled a Danube dispute with Hungary at the World Court, and joined NATO, the European Union and the euro.
body: The Slovak Republic that emerged in 1993 set about building democratic institutions and a market economy, and after a turbulent first decade it secured its place in the Western community of states. It joined NATO on 29 March 2004 and the European Union on 1 May 2004, and on 1 January 2009 it adopted the euro, becoming the sixteenth member of the euro area, a notably swift integration for a state barely sixteen years old.

Independence did not erase older quarrels. A long dispute with Hungary over a vast Danube barrage system, agreed in a 1977 treaty between Hungary and Czechoslovakia but suspended by Hungary in 1989, was carried to the International Court of Justice. In its judgment of 25 September 1997 in the Gabčíkovo–Nagymaros Project case the Court found both states in breach of their obligations: Hungary had not been entitled to abandon the works, while Czechoslovakia, succeeded by Slovakia, had not been entitled to put its unilateral 'provisional solution' diversion of the Danube into operation in 1992. The Court ordered the two neighbours to negotiate in good faith, and the case became a landmark in international environmental law.

Internally, the largest unresolved question of identity is the status of the Hungarian minority concentrated in southern Slovakia, a legacy of the thousand years within Hungary and of the borders drawn after 1918. Ethnic Hungarians form the country's largest national minority, and questions of language, schooling and the post-war Beneš decrees have repeatedly strained relations both within Slovakia and with neighbouring Hungary, even as both states share membership of the European Union.

events.title: ICJ judgment on Gabčíkovo–Nagymaros
events.summary: The International Court of Justice ruled in the Gabčíkovo–Nagymaros Project case (Hungary/Slovakia) that both states had breached their obligations over the Danube dam treaty of 1977, ordering them to negotiate in good faith.

events.title: Slovakia joins NATO
events.summary: Slovakia officially became a member of NATO on 29 March 2004, part of the largest single enlargement in the Alliance's history, anchoring the young republic in the Western security community.

events.title: Accession to the European Union
events.summary: Slovakia joined the European Union on 1 May 2004, completing its integration into Western Europe little more than a decade after independence.

events.title: Slovakia adopts the euro
events.summary: Slovakia adopted the euro on 1 January 2009, becoming the sixteenth member of the euro area and the first of the Visegrád states to do so.

figures.name: Michal Kováč
figures.blurb: Elected by parliament in 1993 as the first head of state of the new Slovak Republic, Kováč defended the country's fledgling democratic institutions during the politically turbulent early years of independence.

figures.name: Andrej Kiska
figures.blurb: A philanthropist elected president in 2014, Kiska led Slovakia during a period of full integration into the European Union and the euro area and became a prominent voice for the rule of law.

pullquote.text: In its 1997 Gabčíkovo–Nagymaros judgment the World Court found both Hungary and Slovakia in breach over the Danube and ordered them to negotiate in good faith.

figures.name: Saints Cyril and Methodius
figures.blurb: Byzantine missionaries who came to Great Moravia in 863, created the Glagolitic script and Old Church Slavonic liturgy, and founded the written culture from which Slovak and other Slavic literacies descend.

figures.name: Ľudovít Štúr
figures.blurb: Leader of the nineteenth-century national awakening who codified the modern Slovak literary language from the central dialects in the 1840s, giving the nation the linguistic foundation of its identity.

figures.name: Milan Rastislav Štefánik
figures.blurb: Slovak astronomer, general and diplomat who helped win Allied recognition for Czechoslovakia in the First World War, becoming one of the new state's founding fathers.

figures.name: Alexander Dubček
figures.blurb: The Slovak communist leader behind the 1968 reform movement and an emblem of the 1989 Velvet Revolution, who chaired the federal parliament as Czechoslovakia returned to democracy.

sources.label: The Slovak National Uprising of 1944
sources.publisher: The National WWII Museum, New Orleans
